html:
The world order today is profoundly shaped by the escalating direct military confrontations between the US and Iran, which have severely tested a fragile interim peace agreement and prompted threats of widespread violence. Both nations have accused each other of violating the ceasefire, leading to retaliatory strikes across the Middle East.

US-Iran Tensions: The interim peace agreement between the US and Iran appears increasingly jeopardized following new rounds of strikes. Iran launched drone and missile attacks targeting Bahrain and Kuwait in response to US airstrikes on its territory, with Washington also announcing further strikes. Amidst these hostilities, Donald Trump threatened that Iran "will no longer exist," while IRGC media stated Iran "must develop nuclear bomb" despite a pledge to Trump. The UAE has condemned the Iranian strikes, and discussions between Foreign Minister Araghchi and Iraqi Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi highlighted efforts for regional stability, though Araghchi emphasized that only Tehran would restore pre-war Hormuz traffic. Reports indicate Iraq has arrested 47 officials in an anti-corruption crackdown, some reportedly tied to Iran-aligned parties.

Russia-Ukraine Conflict: Ukraine has intensified its strategy to bring the war to the Russian people, launching deep drone strikes up to 1,200 miles into Russia, successfully igniting major oil refineries and hitting fuel hubs. Strategic studies professor Phillips O'Brien suggests Ukraine may have gained a localized advantage in modern combat power. Meanwhile, the European Commission has proposed withdrawing protection status for military-age Ukrainian males, a move supported by several EU countries and Kyiv.

European Challenges: A severe heatwave continues to grip Europe, with France reporting approximately 1,000 excess deaths as temperatures of at least 35C affect vast populations. In the UK, households face the steepest rise in summer energy bills in four years, with a 13% increase from July 1, leading to calls for ministers to curb costs. Politically, Labour's deputy leader Lucy Powell has indicated Ed Miliband would be a "good" chancellor to Andy Burnham, the probable next prime minister, who has also been urged by a former head of the armed forces to boost defence spending to meet a "Moscow test."

US Political Landscape: The American domestic political arena remains highly charged, with Joe Biden calling Donald Trump "a loser" in a pugnacious speech, portraying him as incompetent, corrupt, and vain, as Democrats aim to wrest control of Congress in November's midterm elections. Adding to the internal Republican divisions, out-going senator Bill Cassidy accused Trump of treating Congress as "merely an appendage" in his handling of the Iran war, following a face-to-face row over briefing failures.

Global Incidents & Humanitarian Concerns: In Venezuela, rescue teams are racing against time, entering a critical fourth day of searching for survivors after twin earthquakes killed over 1,400 people and left tens of thousands unaccounted for; two boys were rescued after days trapped in rubble, and Syria has sent a rescue team. Separately, a Saudi Aramco helicopter crash in Ras Tanura killed all 14 Saudi nationals on board. In Uganda, the army chief ordered the shutdown of two leading media outlets, declaring he does "not believe in a free press," drawing concerns over press freedom.
